chunk_summary: This section addresses breast engorgement and areolar edema management, focusing on restoring milk flow, reducing edema, and improving latch during breastfeeding to prevent complications like mastitis.
learning_objectives: Understand the causes and symptoms of breast engorgement and areolar edema during lactation.
Learn effective management strategies, including reverse pressure softening and cold compresses.
Recognize the importance of frequent feeding and proper latch to prevent complications like mastitis.
Identify when reassessment is needed for persistent severe engorgement.
key_facts: Engorgement occurs during lactogenesis stage 2 due to interstitial edema and milk accumulation.
Delayed initiation, infrequent feeding, poor latch, or unnecessary supplementation are common causes of engorgement.
Management includes frequent feeding, gentle hand expression, reverse pressure softening, and cold compresses after feeds.
Warmth before feeding can improve milk flow, while NSAIDs can be used for pain relief.
Avoid aggressive deep massage as it may worsen tissue injury.
reference_quote: Engorgement is often driven by delayed initiation, infrequent feeding, poor latch, or unnecessary supplementation. (ABM Clinical Protocol #20: Engorgement)
document_key_topics: Preventing mastitis through effective breastfeeding practices
